examples.title: Contiguous File Allocation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm for allocating files contiguously in disk blocks
examples.code:
ALGORITHM AllocateContiguous(filename, size)
BEGIN
  startBlock ← -1
  freeCount ← 0
  FOR each block FROM diskStart TO diskEnd DO
    IF free[block] == FREE THEN
      freeCount ← freeCount + 1
      IF freeCount == 1 THEN
        startBlock ← block
      END IF
      IF freeCount == size THEN
        BREAK
      END IF
    ELSE
      freeCount ← 0
      startBlock ← -1
    END IF
  END FOR

  IF startBlock == -1 THEN
    RETURN FAILURE // No consecutive space found
  END IF

  FOR i FROM 0 TO size-1 DO
    free[startBlock + i] ← ALLOCATED
  END IF

  createFileEntry(filename, startBlock, size)
  RETURN SUCCESS
END
examples.explanation: Searches disk for consecutive free blocks equal to required size. If found, marks them as allocated and creates file entry with starting block and length.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of disk blocks

examples.title: Linked Allocation File Read
examples.description: Reading a file using linked allocation where blocks are chained
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ReadFileLinked(fileDescriptor, buffer, startPosition, bytesToRead)
BEGIN
  currentBlock ← fileDescriptor.startBlock
  offset ← 0
  bytesRead ← 0

  // Skip to starting position
  WHILE offset < startPosition DO
    currentBlock ← FAT[currentBlock].nextBlock
    offset ← offset + BLOCK_SIZE
  END WHILE

  // Read requested bytes
  WHILE bytesRead < bytesToRead AND currentBlock != EOF DO
    diskData ← ReadBlockFromDisk(currentBlock)
    copyToBuffer(diskData, buffer, bytesRead)
    bytesRead ← bytesRead + BLOCK_SIZE
    currentBlock ← FAT[currentBlock].nextBlock
  END WHILE

  fileDescriptor.currentPosition ← fileDescriptor.currentPosition + bytesRead
  RETURN bytesRead
END
examples.explanation: Follows chain of pointers in FAT to access blocks sequentially. Must traverse through all previous blocks to reach random positions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for accessing nth block, O(1) for sequential after positioned
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) besides buffer and FAT cache

examples.title: Indexed Allocation Block Access
examples.description: Direct access to any file block using index block
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ReadFileIndexed(fileDescriptor, buffer, blockNumber)
BEGIN
  // Get the index block for this file
  indexBlock ← fileDescriptor.indexBlock

  // Read the index block into memory
  indexTable ← ReadBlockFromDisk(indexBlock)

  // Direct access to target block using index
  targetBlock ← indexTable[blockNumber]

  IF targetBlock == INVALID THEN
    RETURN ERROR // Block doesn't exist
  END IF

  // Read the actual data block
  data ← ReadBlockFromDisk(targetBlock)

  copyToBuffer(data, buffer, 0)
  fileDescriptor.currentPosition ← blockNumber * BLOCK_SIZE
  RETURN BLOCK_SIZE
END
examples.explanation: Uses the index block to directly access any file block. Index block contains array of data block pointers, enabling O(1) random access to any block.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for any block access (one extra I/O for index)

examples.title: Bit Vector Free Space Management
examples.description: Implementation of free space management using bit vector (bitmap)
examples.explanation: Implements bitmap-based free space management with methods to allocate consecutive blocks and deallocate them. Maintains a list where each element represents a disk block's allocation status.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for allocation search, O(k) for deallocation where k is blocks to free
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is total disk blocks

examples.title: Directory Entry Structure
examples.description: Data structure for directory entries mapping filenames to inodes
examples.code:
STRUCTURE DirectoryEntry
BEGIN
  character[256] fileName      // File name (max 255 chars)
  integer        inodeNumber  // Pointer to inode in inode table
  character[4]   fileType     // Regular, directory, special
  integer        entryLength // Total length of this entry
  integer        nameLength  // Actual name length
END STRUCTURE

ALGORITHM CreateDirectoryEntry(parentDir, fileName, inodeNum)
BEGIN
  // Check if filename already exists in directory
  FOR each entry IN parentDir.entries DO
    IF entry.fileName == fileName THEN
      RETURN ERROR // File already exists
    END IF
  END FOR

  // Create new directory entry
  newEntry.fileName ← fileName
  newEntry.inodeNumber ← inodeNum
  newEntry.fileType ← "REGULAR"
  newEntry.nameLength ← length(fileName)
  newEntry.entryLength ← SIZE_OF(DirectoryEntry)

  // Add to parent directory
  AppendToDirectory(parentDir, newEntry)
  RETURN SUCCESS
END

ALGORITHM LookupFile(parentDir, fileName)
BEGIN
  FOR each entry IN parentDir.entries DO
    IF entry.fileName == fileName THEN
      RETURN entry.inodeNumber
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN NOT_FOUND
END
examples.explanation: Directory entries store mappings between human-readable filenames and inode numbers that the OS uses to access file metadata and data blocks. Lookup searches entries to find matching filename.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for lookup in linear directory, O(log n) with indexed structure
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per lookup besides directory storage
